question.text: Vintage year analysis in AIF performance evaluation refers to:

answer.text: Analyzing the age of the fund manager
Comparing funds launched in the same year to account for market cycle effects on returns
Evaluating the oldest investments in the portfolio

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Vintage year is the year the fund was launched/started investing. Comparing funds of the same vintage year ensures fair comparison — all faced similar market conditions for entry and exit. A 2015 vintage fund should be compared with other 2015 vintage funds, not 2008 or 2020 vintage. **The Trap:** Comparing funds across different vintage years is misleading. A fund launched in 2009 (post-crisis bargains) will likely show higher returns than one launched in 2007 (pre-crisis peak), regardless of manager skill. **Cross-Exam Context:** Vintage year analysis is a key benchmarking concept tested in XIX-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When presenting track records, always mention the vintage year. Help investors understand that market timing significantly impacts absolute returns. The manager's value-add should be measured against vintage year peers, not absolute return targets.
